text: AUDINAC, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Ariege, cant. of St. Lizier, 3 m. NE of St. Girons. It possesses some mineral springs and baths. Pop. 348.

AUDINCOURT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Doubs, arrond. of Montbeliard. The cant. comprises 23 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 9,659. The town lies on the r. bank of the Doubs river, 4 m. SE of Montbeliard. It possesses a Protestant consistorial church, extensive iron-works, a cambric manufactory, and a cotton spinning-mill. Pop. 1,265.
AUDLAM, or AUDLEM, a parish in Cheshire, 7 m. S by E of Nantwich. Area 11,780 acres. Pop. 2,827, of whom 1,621 were in the township.
AUDLEY, a parish and township in Staffordshire, 4 m. NW of Newcastle-under-Lyne. Area of p. 8,140 acres. Pop. 4,474, of whom 934 were in the township of A.
AUDORF, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Isar, on the r. bank of the Inn, about 5 m. NNE of Kufstein.
AUDRAIN, a county in the E part of the state of Missouri, U. S., watered by the S fork of Salt river. Pop. 1,949.

AUDRUICQ, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, arrond. of St. Omer. The cant. comprises 13 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 13,337. The town is about 10 m. SE of Calais, on the road to St. Omer, and possesses considerable manufactures of lace. Pop. 2,287.

AUDUN-LE-ROMAN, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Moselle, arrond. of Briey. The cant. comprises 35 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,664. The v. lies 21 m. NW of Metz, and possesses manufactures of fire-arms and china. Pop. 409.
AUDUN-LE-TICHE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Moselle, cant. of Audun-le-Roman. Pop. 671.

AUE, a village of Saxony, in the circle of Zwickau, 5 m. NW of Schwarzenberg, on the Mulda river. Pop. in 1834, 1,106. Iron and porcelain clay are procured in the neighbourhood.—Also two rivers in Hanover, one a tributary of the Aller, and the other uniting with the Oste, at Neuhaus, a little above its confluence with the Elbe.—Also a river of Denmark, in Holstein, uniting with the Elbe at Elmshorn.
AUENHEIM, a village of Baden, in the Middle Rhine circle, on the Rhine. Pop. 814.
AUENHEIM (GREAT), a town of Hesse, 2 m. SE of Hanau, on the r. bank of the Main. Pop. 1,120.

AUERBACH, a town of Bavaria, 6 m. SW of Zumbach, on an affluent of the Pegnitz. Pop. 1,571.— Also a village of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the prov. of AUG Stackenburg, 14 m. S of Darmstadt. Pop. 1,574.— Also a town of Saxony, in the circle of Zwickau, 10 m. E of Plauen, on the Gölzsch, at an alt. of 1,722 ft. Pop. 2,985. Lace, muslins, potash, and paper are manufactured here.
AUERSBERG, a town of Austria, in Carniola, 20 m. WNW of Neustadt. Pop. 3,000. The fine old castle of the counts of A., built in 1570, invites attention here. The Wendish name of the town is TRIACK.
AUERSCHITZ, a town of Austria, in Moravia, 4 m. NW of Auspitz, on the l. bank of the Schwarzawa. Pop. 983.

AUESTADT, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, 8 m. W of Naumburg, 22 m. NE of Erfurt, near the junction of the Ilm with the Saall. Pop. 700. On the 14th of October, 1806, the French defeated the Prussians in the neighbourhood of this v.
AUFFAY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Totes, 12 m. S of Dieppe. Pop. 1,137. There are tan-works and bleacheries here.
AUFHAUSEN, a town of Bavaria, in the Rezat circle, NW of Nordlingen. Pop. 386.—Also a town of Wirtemburg, in the Jaxt circle. Pop. 739, of whom nearly one-half are Jews.
AUFKIRCHEN, a village of Bavaria, in the Isar circle; also another v. in the Rezat circle, on the Wernitz. Pop. 510.
AUGAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Guer. Pop. 1,727.
AUGE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, cant. of Saint-Maixent. Pop. 1,366.

AUGELA, or Augila, an oasis and town of Tripoli, to the S of Barca, on the route from Siwah to Fezzan, in about 28° 30' N lat. The district produces excellent dates. The town is small and mean; but is supposed to be on the site of a celebrated temple of Ammon. It afforded a convenient station for the trade which the Cyrenians carried on with Central Africa.
AUGEROLLES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Compiere. Pop. 3,522.
AUGGEN, a village of Baden, in the Upper Rhine circle, 2 m. SSW of Mullheim. Pop. 1,232. Excellent wines are made here.
AUGGUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, 40 m. NE of Oujein, at an alt. of 1,590 ft. above sea-level. It contains about 5,000 houses, and is surrounded by a stone wall.
AUGHA, a parish in co. Carlow. Area 4,184 acres. Pop. 2,027. It stretches along the E side of the river Barrow.
AUGHADRESDAN, a boggy district on the N border of co. Roscommon, comprising about 3,406 acres of bogs and marshes.
AUGHAGOUR, a parish in co. Mayo, a considerable portion of which lies in the basin of the Ayle. It has an area of 55,048 acres. Pop. in 1831, 11,963; in 1841, 12,235, of whom 176 were in the v. of A.
AUGHALOO, a parish in co. Tyrone. Area 19,583 acres. Pop. 9,867. The Blackwater, skirting this p., divides it from co. Armagh. It contains the town of Caledon.
AUGHANAGH, a parish in co. Sligo, 5 m. NW of Boyle. Area 8,839 acres. Pop. 2,714.
AUGHANUNCON, a parish in co. Donegal, on the banks of Lough Swilly. Area 4,012 acres. Pop. 1,649.

AUGHAVAL, a parish in co. Mayo. Area 33,695 acres. Pop. 13,441. The mountain of Croaghpatrick, 61 m. WSW of Westport, alt. 2,530 ft., is in this p.; and 4 m. to the W of this mountain is Knockaskiheez, alt. 1,288 ft., and Oughty, alt. 1,104 ft. Two lakes, respectively on the S, and near the centre of the p., have an alt. of 791 ft. and 592 ft. above sealevel. This parish contains the town of Westport and Westport-Quay.
AUGHAVEA, a parish in co. Fermanagh, on the Colebrook river. Area 17,142 acres. Pop. in 1841, 6,730. It contains the v. of Brooksborough.
AUGHAVILLER, a parish in co. Kilkenny, 23 m. SW of Knocktopher. Area 5,671 acres. Pop. 1,997. It contains the villages of Huggunstown and Boolyglass.
AUGHELOGUN, a village in the p. of Annaghdown, 81 m. NNE of Galway. Pop. 200.
AUGHER, or AGHER, a small town in the p. of Clogher, co. Tyrone, 2 m. NE of Clogher. Pop. 753.
AUGHMACART, a parish in Queen's co., 43 m. SW of Castle-Durrow. Area 9,601 acres. Pop. 3,667. It is watered by the Erkinagh. Its highest hill is 914 ft. above sea-level.
AUGHNACLOY, a market-town in the p. of Carrenteel, co. Tyrone, on the l. bank of the Blackwater, 101 m. N of Monaghan. Pop. 1,841.
AUGHNAMULLEN, a parish in co. Monaghan, 2½ m. S of Ballybay. Area 30,710 acres. Pop. 18,219.
AUGHNISH, a parish in co. Donegal, on the S side of Ramelton bay. Area 9,195 acres. Pop. 4,974. It comprises the town of Ramelton.
AUGHRIM, or AGHRIM, a river in co. Wicklow, one of the three head-streams of the Avoca, which rises on the skirts of Lugnaquilla.
AUGHRIM. See AGHRIM.

AUGHTON, a chapelry in the p. of Halton, in Lancashire, 7 m. NE of Lancaster. Area 1,900 acres. Pop. 134.—Also a p. in West Derby hund., Lancashire, 2½ m. SW of Ormskirk. Area 4,410 acres. Pop. 1,560.—Also a p. in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 8 m. NNW of Howden. Area 4,200 acres. Pop. 634.
AUGIGNIAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Montron. Pop. 1,143.

AUGIREIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ariege, cant. of Castillon. Pop. 639. AUGSBURG,—the Augusta Vindelicorum of the Romans,—an ancient free imperial city of Swabia, now incorporated with the kingdom of Bavaria, and the cap. of the circle of the Upper Danube; situated in a beautiful plain near the confluence of the Lech, the ancient Licus, and the Werlach, the ancient Vindo, about 40 m. NW of Munich, and 300 m. W of Vienna, in N lat. 48° 22', E long. 10° 54'. A. has declined in importance ever since the trade of Germany forsook the direction which Venice in her better days had given it. The rise of the Dutch commonwealth, and the increasing influence of cities like Hamburg, Bremen, Emden, and Frankfort, better situated for carrying on that commerce which now took a westerly direction, instead of continuing in the ancient tract, have proved injurious to this city. Yet A. is still, in spite of every disadvantage, a place of some consideration, and conducts a great part of the transit and banking business of Bavaria, Swabia, and the adjoining districts. There are indeed no Fuggers or Welsers now to be found in it, to lend their emperor millions of dollars on demand; nor is A. any longer the great resort of the literati of Southern Germany. As it now constitutes a part of Bavaria, and is in every respect as dependent upon that crown as if it had never enjoyed the privileges of a free imperial city, it is moreover needless to describe a constitution and police once much talked of in Germany, but now no more. Its streets, with the exception of the Maximilian-strasse, are inconveniently narrow. The Rath-haus, or townhall, and its noble apartment the Golden hall, now a picture gallery, 110 ft. long, 48 broad, and 52 high; the large square, of which it forms a part; the cathedral, with its two tall spires; the arsenal; the public foun- AUG tains, some of them said to have been built by Cæsar Augustus; the intricate gate towards the Lech; and the aqueduct which carries the water of that river into the city, in sufficient quantity to supply some mills -are often mentioned with admiration by statistical writers and travellers. The town is intersected by four canals. The charitable establishments are numerous, and richly endowed, and it has several educational establishments, and a public library of 30,000 volumes. The manufactures are still considerable, though greatly inferior to what they were before the incorporation of the city with Bavaria. They consist of linen and cotton goods, tobacco or snuff, mirrors, leather, parchment, paper, carpets, soap, printing-types, gold and silver laces, sealing-wax, cordials, plate, jewellery, clocks and watches, and stone - ware; besides dyeing, bleaching, printing, and other branches connected with these manufactures. A. is a staple town for the sale of wines; and its banking and exchange operations are considerable,—the greater part of the transactions between Western Germany and Italy being finally balanced and adjusted here, embracing transactions to the extent of £5,000,000. The prices of the necessaries of life are as low here as in any of the larger commercial towns of the continent; and the surrounding country is beautiful and fertile.—The pop. of A. has, since 1788, fluctuated betwixt 30,000 and 36,000 souls, of whom two-thirds are Roman Catholics, and the rest Protestants. Jews were not, until 1805, allowed to live within the walls; but such as carried on business in A. lodged in a village halfa-league from the city, and paid a certain tax for liberty of trading in A. through the day. It is the see of a Catholic bishop, a suffragan of Munich. History.] A. was once a very flourishing, large, and handsome city, a principal seat in Germany of commerce, manufactures, and the liberal arts. Its position was highly favourable for trade between Italy, Tyrol, Switzerland, and the northern states; and accordingly it was for many ages the grand entrepot of the commerce carried on between the Venetian territories and the different provinces of Germany, Bohemia, and the various states which extend from the gulf of Venice to the shores of the Baltic. Here was held the celebrated diet of the empire, by Charles V. in person, A. D. 1530, at which the confederate princes, who had a few months before protested aganst the act of the imperial diet of Spires,—which declared any religious innovations rebellion against the Germanic body,—assumed the name of Protestants. At this diet, the well-known Lutheran 'Confession of Faith,'consisting of twenty-eight chapters concerning the nature, reasons, and extent of the separation from the Romish church, drawn up by Melancthon, was presented to the emperor and all the German princes; discussed with great earnestness for many weeks; and finally adopted as the code of Lutheran Protestantism in Germany. It was not merely the Roman Catholic party, however, to whom the proceedings of the diet of A., and the confession of faith as drawn up by Melancthon, gave offence: a very numerous body of the Protestants themselves disapproved of the whole transaction, assumed the denomination of 'Evangelical Reformed,' and are still known by that title, and constitute nearly one-third of the Protestants of all Germany. The religious affairs of the empire remained in great confusion for twenty-five years after the dissolution of the diet of A. in 1530. Ferdinand of Austria, brother of Charles V., for whom the emperor had procured the dignity of king of the Romans, sincerely wishing an accommodation with the Protestants, called together a new diet at A. in 1555, and contrived to establish something like a religious peace in Germany. A Recess was framed on the 25th of Sept. 1555, approved of, and published with the usual formalities. It contained, among other articles, the following, which we give as a specimen of the religious toleration of the age. "Such princes and cities as have declared their approbation of the confession of A., in 1530, shall be permitted to profess the doctrine and exercise the worship which it authorises, without interruption or molestation from the emperor, the king of the Romans, or any power or person whatsoever. The Protestants, on their part, shall give no disquiet to the princes and states who adhere to the tenets and rites of the church of Rome. For the future, no attempts shall be made towards terminating religious differences, but by the gentle and pacific methods of persuasion and conference. The popish ecclesiastics shall claim no spiritual jurisdiction in such states as receive the confession of Augsburg. Such as held the revenues or benefices of the church, previous to the treaty of Passau, shall retain possession of them, and be liable to no prosecution in the imperial chamber on that account. The supreme civil power in every state shall have right to establish what form of doctrine and worship it shall deem proper; and if any of its subjects refuse to conform to these, shall permit them to remove with all their effects whithersoever they shall please. If any prelate or ecclesiastic shall hereafter abandon the Romish religion, he shall instantly relinquish his diocese, or benefice; and it shall be lawful for those in whom the right of nomination is vested, to proceed immediately to an election, as if the office were vacant by death or translation, and to appoint a successor of undoubted attachment to the ancient system." The Recess of A., it is evident, was founded on no very liberal or enlarged sentiments concerning freedom of religious enquiry, or the nature of toleration. It was nothing more than a scheme of pacification, which political considerations alone suggested to the contending parties, and regard for their mutual tranquillity and safety rendered necessary. Of this there can be no stronger proof than an article in the Recess itself, by which the benefits of the pacification are declared to extend only to the Catholics on the one side, and to such as adhered to the confession of A., in 1530, on the other. The followers of Zuinglius and Calvin remained, in consequence of that exclusion, without any protection from the rigour of the laws against heretics. Nor did they obtain any legal security until the treaty of Westphalia, in 1648, provided, that they should be admitted to enjoy all the advantages and protection which the recess of A. affords.
AUGST, or Kaiser-Augst, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Argau, on the r. bank of the Ergolz, at its junction with the Rhine. Pop. 323, all Catholics.

AUGST, or Basle-Augst, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Basle, on the I. bank of the Ergolz, opposite the above v., about 5 m. ESE of Basle, and 4 m. NW of Liestal. The only manufacture is paper. Pop. 367, all Protestants.—This and the preceding village occupy the site of the ancient Augusta Rauracorum, which was destroyed by the Huns in 450, and of which the ruins of an amphitheatre still exist. Upwards of 12,000 Roman coins and medals found in this locality were bequeathed by Erasmus to the Literary institution of Basle.
AUGST, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Zurich, and district of Affoltern, near which there is a sulphureous spring and baths.

AUGSTHALZBAD, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Lucerne, pleasantly situated near the Baldeker-see. Its baths render it a place of considerable resort. AUGUR, - formerly denominated GHEPP, or DUN GHEPP,—a district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Lahore, extending between the 22d and 23d parallels N, from the Indus on the W to the Jehun or Hydaspes on the E; and bounded on the S by the Sinde Sager. This territory is intersected by ranges of salt-mountains, and the soil generally is salinaceous. It contains no towns of importance.

AUGUSTA, the capital of the state of Maine, U. S., in Kennebec co. It is situated on both sides of the Kennebec river, at the head of the sloopnavigation, 43 m. from the ocean, in N lat. 44° 19', W long. 69° 50'; 146 m. NE of Concord. Pop. in 1810, 1,805; in 1840, 5,314. The two sections of the town are connected by a bridge 520 ft. in length. The principal buildings are the state-house; the United States' arsenal; the state asylum for the insane; and the high-school. An immense dam is thrown across the Kennebec half-a-mile above the town; the lake or pond formed by this dam covers 1,200 acres.—Also a township in Oneida co., state of New York, 100 m. WNW of Albany. Pop. 2,175.— Also a township in Northumberland co., Pennsylvania, 65 m. N of Harrisburg. Pop. 2,409. It is watered by the Great and Little Shamokin creeks.- Also a township in Carroll co., state of Ohio, 135 m. E of Columbus, watered by tributaries of Shandy creek. Pop. 1,234.—Also a village in Des Moines co., state of Iowa, on the N side of Skunk river.- Also a village in Bracken co., state of Kentucky, 73 m. NE of Frankfort. Pop. 786.—Also a county in the W part of Virginia, watered by tributaries of the James river and Shenandoah. Area 900 sq. m. Pop. in 1830, 19,925; in 1840, 19,628. Wheat and Indian corn are the principal productions of the soil. Its cap. is Staunton.—Also the capital of Richmond * AUK co. Georgia. It is situated on the SW side of Savannah river, 96 m. from Milledgeville, and 120 m. NW of Savannah. The streets run rectangularly, and are adorned with trees. The principal buildings are the city-hall, court-house, jail, theatre, and arsenal. The surrounding country is fertile, and the trade in cotton, tobacco, and other produce, is considerable. Pop. in 1830, 4,000; in 1840, 6,403.—Also a town in Kalamazoo co., state of Michigan, on the S bank of the Kalamazoo river, and 12 m. E of the town of that name. Pop. 646.—Also a town of Washtenau co., in Michigan. Pop. 646.
AUGUSTA. See Agosta.
AUGUSTENBORG, a town of Denmark, in Sleswick, on the W coast of the isle of Als. Pop. 600.
AUGUSTIN (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. and cant. of Correze. Pop. 1,125. -Also a commune and village in the dep. of Seineet-Marne, cant. of Coulommiers. Pop. 1,447.

AUGUSTINE (SAINT), the cap. of St. John's co., in the state of Florida, U.S.; 200 m. E by S of Tallahassee, and 880 m. from Washington. It is situated in N lat. 29° 52', W long. 81° 25', 2 m. from the sea-coast, on the S point of a peninsular projection of the mainland, fronting E on Matanzas sound; on ground scarcely 12 ft. above sea-level. The houses are generally built of stone, and the streets are regularly laid out. Pop. 2,500. The harbour has 18 or 20 ft. water and 9 ft. over the bar.
AUGUSTINE (SAINT), an island of the Loochoo group, in N lat. 24° 15', E long. 141° 22'. It is a volcanic mountain, 1½ m. in diam., and rising to the alt. of 3,300 ft.
AUGUSTINE (CAPE SAN). See AGOSTINHO.

AUGUSTINE'S (SAINT), a river and port on the coast of Labrador, on the N side of the gulf of St. Lawrence, opposite St. John's bay, in N lat. 51° 10'. A chain of small islands, called Augustine's chain, lies to the SW of the harbour, its southern extremity bearing W & S, 7 m. from Shag island.

AUGUSTOW, or Augustova, a province of Russian Poland, lying between the parallels of 52° 40' and 55° 5' N lat. Its area is 17,875 sq. kilometres. Pop. in 1836, 562,381. A great part of the surface is covered with lakes, marshes, and immense forests. The exports consist of grain, wood, lint, honey, butter, cheese, and fish. The prov. is divided into five oboody, viz., Lomza, Augustow, Seigny, Kalwarya, and Mariampol. It comprises 47 towns, and 4,471 villages and hamlets. The cap. is a town of the same name, on the Netta, an affluent of the Narew, 20 m. S of Suwalki. Pop. 3,350.

AUGUSTUS (FORT), the central of three forts erected in the Highlands of Scotland since the Revolution. It is at the W extremity of Loch Ness, in the parish of Boleskine, Inverness-shire, 324 m. SW of Inverness. It is a regular fortification, with 4 bastions, a ditch, covert-way, and glacis, and barracks for 300 men. The Caledonian canal here descends, by five locks, into Loch Ness.
AUGY-SUR-ARBOIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cher, cant. of Sancoins. Pop. 978.
AUHAUSEN, a town of Bavaria, on the Wernitz, 4 m. from Ottingen. Pop. 409. The 'Evangelical Union' was concluded here in 1608.
AUJAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Saint-Hilaire. Pop. 1,030.
AUJEST, a town of Austria, on the Bohemian circle of Leitmaritz, 25 m. N of Prague.-Also a v. of Bohemia, 7 m. N of Politza.
AUJON, a river of France, an affluent of the Aube, which it joins on the r. bank near Clairvaux, after a course of 40 m.
AUKBOROUGH, a parish in Lincolnshire, in the N division of Manley wapentake. Area 2,540 acres. Pop. 528.
AUKLAND. See AUCKLAND.
AULA (LOWER), a town of Hessen-Cassel, in the prov. of Fulda, at the confluence of the Aula and Fulda, 8 m. SW of Hersfeld. Pop. 1,030.
AULA (UPPER), a town of Hessen-Cassel, in the prov. of Upper Hessen, 10 m. SE of Ziegenhain, on the Aula. Pop. 810.
AULAIRE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Correze, cant. of Ayen. Pop. 1,198.
AULAONG, a town in the Birman empire, upon the Numlung river, 25 m. N of Taungdut, in N lat. 23° 59′.
AULAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Le Vigan. Pop. 1,000.
AULAYE (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, arrond. of Riberac. The cant. comprises 13 com. The com. and v., 8 m. WSW of Riberac, has a pop. of 1,450.

AULDEARN, a parish in Nairnshire, on the Moray frith. Pop. in 1831, 1,613; in 1841, 1,466, of whom 351 were in the v. of A., which is 2½ m. SE of Nairn. In May 1645, Montrose defeated the Covenanting army under Hussy at this v.
AULEBEN, a town of Prussian Saxony, in the circle of Merseburg. Pop. 758.
AULENDORF, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, 12 m. N of Ravensburg. Pop. 1,000. Alt. above sea-level 1,953 ft.
AULELLA, a river of Italy, in Modena, an affluent of the Magra, which it joins on the l. bank, at Aulla, after a course of 20 m.
AULETTA, a town of Naples, in Principato Citra, on the Negro river, in the district of Sala. Pop. 2,863.
AULLA, a town of Modena, in the delegation of Lunigiana, 18 m. NW of Massa-di-Carrara, at the junction of the Aulella with the Magra. Pop. 790. A rich abbey was founded here in 884.
AULLAGAS (Das), a lake of Bolivia, which is connected with Lake Titicaca, and is apparently on a lower level, but has no outlet.
AULLENE, a town of Corsica, in the cant. of 1,020. Serra, on the Talaro river, 10 m. E of Ajaccio. Pop. 1,033.

AULNAY, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, arrond. of Saint- Jean-d'Angely.—The cant. comprises 23 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,223.—The com., 8 m. E by N of Saint- Jean-d'Angely, has a pop. of 1,520.

AULNAY-SUR-ODON, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados, arrond. of Vire. -The cant., comprising 19 com., had a pop. of 13,397 in 1831.—The com. and town, 14 m. N by E of Vire, have a pop. of 2,000.

AULNE, or AUNE, a river of France, in the dep. of Finisterre, which after a winding course of 70 m., in which it passes Chateauneuf and Chateaulin, falls into the harbour of Brest at Landevenec. It is navigable with the tide to Chateaulin, a distance of 14 m., and makes a part of the line of water-communication between Nantes and Brest.
AULNOIS-EN-BANOIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. of Ancerville. Pop. 514.
AULNOY, a commune of France, in the dep. of E long. 14° 2'. Nord, cant. and 2 m. S of Valenciennes. Pop. 1,143. Beet-root sugar is manufactured here.
AULON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Garonne, cant. of Aurignac. Pop. 1,268.

AULT, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Somme, arrond. of Abbeville. - The cant., comprising 19 com., has a pop. of 12,521.—The com. and v., 14 m. W of Abbeville, on the sea-coast, has a - AUP pop. of 1,450. The inhabitants are chiefly occupied in fishing for the supply of the Paris market.
AULTGRANDE, a bold rapid stream in Rossshire, by which Loch Glass discharges its waters into the frith of Cromarty.
AULT-HUCKNALL, a parish of Derbyshire, 5 m. NW of Mansfield. Area 3,730 acres. Pop. 678.
AULUS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ariege, cant. of Oust. Pop. 897.
AUMA, a town of Sachsen-Weimar, in the circle and 8 m. ESE of Neustadt, on a stream of the same name, an affluent of the Elster. Pop. 1,600. Cotton and woollen manufactures exist here.

AUMALE, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Neufchatel.—The cant., comprising 13 com., had a pop. of 8,346 in 1831.—The com. and v. are on the Bresle, 15 m. E by N of Neufchatel. Pop. 1,980. Lace, woollens, and leather are extensively manufactured here.—The dukedom of A., created in 1547, passed in 1638 to a branch of the House of Savoy, and was bestowed in 1675 on Prince Louis-Auguste de Bourbon of France.
AUMESSAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Alzon. Pop. 953.
AUMETZ, a commune of France, in the dep. of Moselle, cant. of Audun-le-Roman. Pop. 1,173.

AUMONT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Jura, cant. of Poligny. Pop. 962.—Also a com. in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Senlis. Pop. 270.-Also a cant. and com. in the dep. of Lozere, arrond. of Marvejols. The cant. comprises 6 com. The com. and v., 10 m. N of Marvejols, had a pop. of 1,002 in 1836.
AUMUTINA, a village of Hindostan, in the Mysore, 20 m. from Peripetam.
AUNALAS, a district of Ecuador, watered by a stream of the same name, which falls into the Amazon in S lat. 3° 55'.
AUNAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nievre, cant. of Chatillon-en-Bazois. Pop. 1,144.
AUNAY-SOUS-AUNEAU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, com. of Auneau. Pop.
AUNAY-SOUS-CRESY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, cant. of Dreux. Pop. 247.
AUNE, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, 15 m. SE of Liege, on the Aywaile.
AUNEAU, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, arrond. of Chartres.—The cant. comprises 28 com.—The com. on the Aunay, 12 m. E of Chartres, had a pop. of 1,616 in 1836.
AUNEUIL, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Oise, arrond. of Beauvais. The cant. comprises 19 com. The com., 5 m. SW of Beauvais, had a pop. of 1,285 in 1836.

AUNIS, an ancient prov. of France, of which La Rochelle was the cap. Its area was 151,871 hectares, of which 125,950 are now comprised in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, and 25,921 in the dep. of Deux-Sevres.
AUNON, a town of Spain in New Castile, in the prov. of Guadalaxara, on the l. bank of the Tagus, 12 m. NE of Almonacid.
AUOUL-MULL, a town of Bornu, in the district of Kanem, 70 m. N of Lake Tchad, in N lat. 15° 6',

AUPA, a river of Bohemia, in the circle of Konigsgratz, which rises in the Riesengeberge, near the Schneekoppe, and after a rapid course of about 40 m., in which it passes Marschendorf and Trautenau, falls into the Elbe, on the l. bank, at Jaromierz.

AUPA (GREAT and LITTLE), two parishes and villages of Bohemia, in the circle of Konigsgratz, of which the former, 5 m. NW of Marschendorf, on the Aupa, has a pop. of 2,426; and the latter, 5 m. N of the same town, on the Little Aupa, a branch of the Aupa, has a pop. of 1,023. Copper and arsenicworks exist in the environs of these villages.
AUPRE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Voiron. Pop. 1,041.
AUPS, or AULPS, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Var. The cant. comprises 6 com. The com. is 12 m. W by N of Draguignan. Pop. 3,080.
AURA, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Lower Maine, on the Saale. Pop. 560.
AURACH, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Rezat, on a stream of the same name, 5 m. SW of Anspach. Pop. 1,604. It was the Uraba of the Middle ages.
AURAS, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, on the Oder, 10 m. NW of Breslau. Pop. in 1837, 896. Potash is largely manufactured here.

AURAY, a canton, commune, and seaport of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, arrond. of Lorient. -The cant., comprising 6 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 13,117. The com. and town, near the mouth of a small river of the same name, 21 m. SE of Lorient, had a pop. of 3,734 in 1841. The commerce of the port consists in cattle, corn, wood, honey, and wax.
AURBACH, a town of Bavaria, in the Upper Danube circle, NE of Memmingen. Pop. 156.—Also a town of Hessen, in the circle of Stackenburg.

AURE, a river of France, in the dep. of Calvados, an affluent of the Vire. It has a course of 20 m., and is navigable at full tide from Trevieres .- Also a river in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, which joins the Eure on the l. at Montreuil, after a course of 30 m.
AUREC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Loire, cant. of Saint-Didier-la-Seauve. Pop. 2,570.
AUREIL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, cant. of Limogen. Pop. 1,150.
AUREILHAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrenees, cant. of Tarbes. Pop. 1,186.
AURELIUS, a township in Cayuga co., in the state of New York, U. S., 5 m. W of Auburn, on the Owasco. Pop. 2,645.—Also a village in Washington co., Ohio, 20 m. N of Marietta.

AURIABAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrenees, cant. of Maubourguet. Pop. 1,060.—Also a com. in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Montignac. Pop. 1,145. - Also a com. in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, cant. of Caraman. Pop. 1,737 .- Also a com. in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of These. Pop. 317.
AURIAC-L'EGLISE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Massiac. Pop. 1,007.

AURICH, a province and town of Hanover.—The prov. comprehends the ancient principality of East Friesland, the territory of Harlinger-land, and some small islands in the German ocean. It has an area of 5,403 German sq. m. Pop. in 1833, 152,408, of whom 101,470 were Lutherans, and 3,111 Catholics. It is divided into 10 royal amts or bailiwicks, viz., Aurich, Berun, Emden, Esens, Friedeburg, Greetsehl, Jemgum, Leer, Norden, Stickhausen, Weener, and Wittmund.—The chief town, of the same name, is 15 m. NE of Emden. Pop. 3,530. It has manufactories of paper and snuff, and frequent cattle-fairs. A canal runs from this town to Emden.

AURIGNAC, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, arrond. of Saint-Gaudens. The cant. comprises 20 com. The com, and v. are 9 m. NE of Saint-Gaudens. Pop. of com. 1,500; of cant., in 1831, 12,453.
AURIGNY. See ALDERNEY.

AURILLAC, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Cantal .- PPP PPP TO PPP TO A CARAGO A CARACA PARAMANANA AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AN AUR The cant., comprising 21 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 31,709.—The arrond., comprising the cantons of Aurillac, Saint-Cernin, Saint-Mamet, Maurs, Montsalvy, Roquebron, and Vic-sur-Cere, had a pop. in 1836 of 98,092; in 1846 of 96,916.—The com. and town are on the r. bank of the Jordanne, in N lat. 44° 56', E long. 2° 26′, 147 m. E of Bourdeaux. Pop. in 1789, 7,956; in 1836, 10,889. The chief manufactures of the town are coarse lace, carpets, leather, and braziery. There is a communal college here; and a public library of 6,000 vols. Pope Sylvester II., Piganol de la Force, and the infamous Carrier, of revolutionary notoriety, were natives of this town.
AURIOL, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, cant. of Roquevaire, 15 m. SE of Aix. Pop. 5,320. It has some woollen manufactures, and brick and tile-works.
AURONZO, a town of Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, 30 m. NNE of Belluno.

AURORA, a township in Erie co., state of New York, U. S., 276 m. W of Albany. Pop. 2,908.— Also a village in Cayuga co., New York, 170 m. W of Albany. Pop. 500.—Also a town in Portage co., Ohio, 144 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 906.—Also one of the New Hebrides islands, in S lat. 15° 8', E long. 167° 48'. It is about 30 m. in length, and 5 m. in breadth; and produces cocoa-nuts, yams, sugarcanes, and bread-fruit.—Also one of the Paumotu group, the N end of which is in S lat. 15° 49' 35", W long. 148° 13′ 15″. This island—sometimes called Metia—seems to be "a coral island uplifted."
AUROS, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde. The cant. comprises 13 com. The com. is 6 m. NNE of Bazas. Pop. 560.

AURUNGABAD, an extensive province of Hindostan, in the territory of the Deccan; bounded on the N by the prov. of Gujerat, Candeish, and Berar; on the E by those of Berar and Hyderabad; on the S by the prov. of Bejapur and Beeder; and on the W by the Indian ocean. It stretches from 18° to 21° N lat., and from 73° to 77° E long.; and is estimated at 300 m. in length, and at about 160 m. in average breadth. It presents considerable irregularity of surface. Towards the W the country is intersected, in a northerly direction, by the lofty range of the Western Ghauts; and a ramification of that chain extends along its northern confines. The central and more easterly regions form a part of the great tableland of the Deccan; and exhibit an average alt. of 1,800 ft. above sea-level. The coast-district is generally flat and sandy. Several large rivers take their rise in this prov. Of these the principal is the Godavery, which issues in several streamlets from the Ghauts, and, running in a general direction ESE, forms the largest rivers of the Deccan. The more southerly districts are watered by the Beema and the Neera. Several rivers of minor extent run towards the W, and fall into the Indian ocean. The coast is much indented, and forms numerous capes and bays, affording excellent harbours. The soil generally is of a highly fertile character; but in many parts fails in productiveness from want of the means of irrigation, —the smaller rivers becoming in summer entirely dry. The climate,—with the exception of that of the district stretching along the coast,-is peculiarly favourable for the culture of European fruits, especially peaches, nectarines, figs, grapes, melons, and strawberries, which are here to be met with in the greatest perfection. The cocoa-nut, sugar, cotton, indigo, rice, and all the varieties of agricultural productions common to the Deccan, are also extensively cultivated. The mountain-districts are covered with wood, affording excellent timber. The rearing of cattle and horses forms an important branch of industry, especially of the Mahratta population. The culture of bees is "PP PP PP PP PP PP PP PP PP PP PP PP PP also extensively carried on. The manufactures of the prov. consist principally in arms, agricultural implements, cotton fabrics, &c. The cities and larger towns present a busy and sufficiently cheerful aspect; but the prevalence of flat roofs and dead walls renders that of the smaller extremely dull. The religious edifices of this portion of the Deccan exhibit varieties of architecture, differing considerably from those of other regions of Hindostan. The principal modern divisions of this prov. are Ahmednuggur, Baglana, Bheer, Bombay, Calliani, Dowlatabad, Futtehabad, Jalnapur, Juner, Jowar, Perramdah, Sholapur, and Singumnere.—This prov. has successively borne the names of the cities which have at different eras been distinguished as its capital. Ahmednuggur was, on the subversion of the Nizam Shahy dynasty by that of the brief acendancy of the Abyssinian, supplanted by Dowlatabad. The present appellation was adopted in the reign of Shah Jehan, when the territory of the Deccan became a subah of the Mogul empire. Since the overthrow of the Mahratta supremacy, in 1818, nearly the entire territory—with the reservation of certain rights by some of the native chiefs-has been gradually subjected to the British power. The pop. consists chiefly of Hindus. The language in prevailing use is the Mahratta, but a variety of local dialects are commonly spoken. The Hindostanee and Persic are confined to the higher classes.

AURUNGABAD, a town of Hindostan, the cap. of the above-mentioned province, situated in N lat. 19° 54', E long. 75° 33'; about 21 m. to the N of the Godavery; and 9 m. ESE of Dowlatabad. It is about 7 m. in circumference; and stands in the midst of an extensive plain, almost entirely surrounded by mountains, from which a constant supply of excellent water is conducted by means of stone conduits to the numerous reservoirs which are distributed throughout the city. The main street or bazaar, which is nearly 2 m. in length, and of proportionate width, is of peculiar architecture, and expands at one extremity into a spacious quadrangle. The shahgunge or market-place, is also of handsome construction. The more ancient edifices are now generally falling into decay. The magnificent palace of Aurungzebe is in ruins, and its once luxuriant gardens are overgrown with tangled brushwood. The celebrated mausoleum erected by the viceroy to the memory of his daughter is also much dilapidated. Of the numerous mosques which still exist, none possess any architectural beauty, and the fakeer's tomb is only remarkable for the magnificence of its water-works. The commerce of A., consisting in articles of local production, and various species of European commodities, is considerable. Since 1815 the pop. has been gradually on the increase; and in 1825 amounted to 60,000. The miasma of the adjacent marshes, in conjunction with the low situation of the city and the generally neglected state of the water-tanks, renders the climate of A. extremely insalubrious. The average temperature of the two last and first months of the year, during which easterly winds prevail, varies often with rapid alternations from 50° to 86°. In the hot months, during which the wind blows without intermission from WSW, the therm. ranges from 78° to 100°. The yearly quantity of rain averages about 36 in. The environs of A. produce tropical fruits of various descriptions in the greatest luxuriance, and rice is extensively grown in the marshy localities. The military cantonments stand in a rocky and remarkably healthy situation, about a mile to the SW of the city. A., originally a miserable village named Gurka, became, on the overthrow of the short-lived dynasty of Mallek Amber, the head-quarters of the Mogul government, and subsequently the favourite residence of the viceroy, from whom it received its present ~ AUS appellation-which signifies 'the place of the throne;' and it continued the metropolis of the Nizams until they removed their seat of government to Hyderabad.
AUSA, a river of Italy, in the States-of-the- Church, which falls into the Adriatic about 2 m. to the E of Rimini.

AU SABLE, a township in Clinton co., in the state of New York, U.S., 15 m. S of Plattsburg, and 140 m. N of Albany. It is generally hilly, and is intersected by the Great and Little Au Sable rivers. The soil consists of sand and clayey loam. Pop. in 1840, 3,222.—Also a river of the state of New York, U. S., which takes its rise in Essex co., and, after passing through a narrow chasm, 2 m. in length and upwards of 100 ft. in average depth, discharges itself into Lake Champlain, in the southern district of Clinton co.
AU SABLE FORKS, a village of New York, U. S., Essex co., at the junction of the E and W branches of the Au Sable river. Pop. 500.
AUSCHE, a village of Austria, in Bohemia, in the circle and 10 m. ENE of Leitmeritz. It possesses some manufactures of cloth. Hops form the only article of commerce. Pop. 1,496.
AUSCHOWITZ, a village of Austria, in Bohemia, in the circle of Pilsen, W of Tepel. In the environs are the celebrated mineral springs and baths of Marienbad.
AUSCHWITZ. See OSWIECIN.

AUSPITZ, a town of Austria, in Moravia, in the circle and 10 m. SSE of Brunn, belonging to the Prince of Lichtenstein. Pop. 2,906. Its wines are highly esteemed, and the environs produce excellent fruit. Its commerce consists in grain, fruit, wine, and cattle.

AUSSA, or Owssa, the chief town of Adel, in Abyssinia, 25 leagues from Tajurrah. It consists of about 1,400 or 1,500 houses, with a pop. of 5,000 or 6,000, chiefly Mudaitus, who are cultivators and traders. The soil in the neighbourhood is productive, and the inhabitants supply Gondar and Central Abyssinia with durrah.

AUSSAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, and cant. of St. Amand-de-Boixe, 13 m. NE of Angouleme. Pop. 874. Turkeys, which are reared in great numbers in this district, form its chief traffic.

AUSSEE, a town of Austria, in the prov. of Styria, in the circle of Judenburg, near the junction of the head-waters of the Traun, about 9 m. NE of Saltzburg, consisting of 676 houses. Pop. 1,176. It possesses numerous manufactures of ironware. In the vicinity are the famous salt-mines of Sandlingberg. The environs afford also gypsum, alabaster, marble, coal, and peat.

AUSSIG, a town of Austria, in Bohemia, in the circle and about 9 m. NNW of Leitmeritz, on the 1. bank of the Elbe, at the junction of the Pala, consisting of about 138 houses. Pop. in 1830, 1,759. It is the seat of a criminal court, and has manufactures of calicoes and paper. Its commerce consists in grain, fruit, wine, timber, and cattle. A. is celebrated as the birth-place of the painter Ant Raphael Mengs; and was the scene of a conflict with the Hussites in 1426.—The adjacent district, which is mountainous, affords coal, fruit, and a species of red wine called Oodshalsky. It is also reputed to contain some varieties of precious stones.
AUSSON. See Auxon.

AUST, a chapelry in the parish of Henbury, Gloucestershire, 4 m. WSW of Thornbury. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. 191. There is a ferry here across the Severn to Chepstow, called the Old passage, to distinguish it from the New passage 2 m. lower; and it has been proposed by the Bristol and Liverpool junction railway to erect a suspension bridge across the river here, with three principal openings of 1,100 ft. each, and an entire waterway of 4,740 ft.

AUSTELL (SAINT), a market-town and parish in Cornwall, 10 m. S by W of Bodmin. Area of p. 11,540 acres. Pop. 10,320. The town has some woollen manufactures; but the greater part of the inhabitants are employed in mining, and in the pilchard-fisheries.
AUSTERFIELD, a chapelry in the parish of Blyth, in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. NNE of Bawtry. Area 2,710 acres. Pop. 314.

AUSTERLITZ, a town in the dominions of the prince of Kaunitz-Rittberg, in the circle of Brünn, in Moravia, 10 m. E by S of Brünn, on the highway which leads by Göding to Hungary, near the Litlawa river. Pop. 2,000.—A. is famous for the great battle of the 2d December, 1805, and the armistice of the 6th of the same month, -events which were turning points in the destiny of Europe and of Napoleon. Their immediate consequence was the peace of Presburg; but the most important result was the subjection of Germany and the humiliation of Prussia; for the victory at A. not only frustrated Pitt's great plan of reducing the power of France, by the allied arms of Britain, Russia, and Austria, to the bounds which, ten years after, the peace at Paris assigned to it, but also established Napoleon's continental and federative system. Napoleon, after the capitulation of Mack, at Ulm, Oct. 19, unchecked at Lambach and Mariazell by the Austrians under Meerveldt, and at Dürnstein, Nov. 11, by the Russians under Kutusoff, occupied Vienna on the 13th of Nov., and immediately took possession of the bridge over the Danube, leading to Moravia, while Prince Auersberg, who should have burnt it, allowed himself to be deluded by a pretended negotiation for peace. Marshal Lannes, on the 15th, came up with the Russian army under Kutusoff, who, to preserve himself, resolved to sacrifice his rearguard of 6,000 men, under Prince Bagration. This intrepid general, however, though attacked by 30,000 French at Hollbrunn, on the 16th, and at Guntersdorf, on the 17th, forced his way to the main army, on the 19th. The Emperor Alexander had arrived, on the 18th, from Berlin; and, on the same day, the second Russian army, under Buxhöfden, had united with that of Kutusoff. On the 24th, the Russian guards, 10,000 strong, arrived; and it was resolved at Olmütz, the head-quarters of the two emperors, Alexander and Francis,—the troops being then in want of provisions—to march from the advantageous station of Olschan (8 m. from A.), in five parallel columns, against Brünn, where Napoleon had taken up his head-quarters, and offer him battle. But the Russians lost many days by repeated changes of their plans of attack, and Napoleon deceived them both by negotiation, and by retiring, as if he wished to avoid an attack. He thus gained time, till the arrival of the corps under Bernadotte, and two divisions of Davoust's corps, Dec. 1st, when he prepared his army, which rested on Brünn, for battle, on the following day, being the anniversary of his coronation. The French army, in a position unknown to Kutusoff, was about 80,000 strong. The army of the allies numbered about 84,000 foot, and 16,000 horse, among which were 20,000 Austrian troops. On the morning of the 2d, about 7 o'clock, the battle began, according to a plan prepared by the Austrian general Weyrotter. Buxhöfden, who commanded the left wing of the Russian army, was stationed beside the 1st column led by Lieut.-general Docktoroff, which, together with the second, under General Langeron, and the 3d, under General Przybyszewsky, was to surround the right wing of the French under Soult. The village of Telnitz, 6 m. SW of A., near the Goldbach rivulet, was taken after an obstinate engagement; but, deceived by the enemy's retreat, Buxhöfden pressed forward, with the 1st column, too far to the left, and fell into a narrow defile which two divisions of Davoust had occupied in the night. About the same time, the 2d and 3d columns, in order to attack the right wing also in front, had left the heights of Pratzen, which overlooked the field of battle, and which were immediately occupied by Soult, and maintained, after a fight of two hours, by the aid of a part of the centre, under Bernadotte, against the efforts of Kutusoff. This decided the victory; for the Russian left wing, which was before engaged with Davoust, and, after Soult's change of position, with the French reserve, was now cut off from the centre, and attacked both in flank and rear. Lannes immediately pressed forward with the left wing, and the French centre, under Bernadotte, supported by a welldirected fire, broke the centre of the allies, composed chiefly of newly-enlisted troops, and pushed it upon the right wing of the Russians under Bagration and Liechtenstein, so that the Russian reserve came too soon into the engagement, though it made for some time a gallant resistance under the Grand Prince Constantine and the Prince Dolgorucki. After this body was thrown into disorder by the French left wing under Lannes, and AUS the last attack of the Russian guards frustrated by the French guards and the cavalry under Murat, the allied army, about 1 o'clock, retreated, under cover of Bagration and Kienmayr, in good order, to A., and, at 4 o'clock, passed over the March. The issue of this battle was singular. The French troops of the right wing, with their rear resting on A., attacked the remainder of the left wing of the allies, and marched down from the same heights from which, in the morning, the allies had descended to attack them. The Russian left wing suffered most, as it had to force its way over the frozen ponds formed by the Litlawa at Kobelnitz and Satschan, and over a narrow dike. According to the French account, several thousand of the allied troops were drowned in these ponds when the ice was broken with shot. At this crisis Przybyszewsky, with 113 officers and 6,000 men, was forced to lay down arms. According to Kutusoff's report, the Russians lost 12,000 men. The French made their own loss about 4,500 men; and the number of prisoners taken on the 2d and 3d, about 20,000 men, and of cannons-which, for the most part, stuck fast in the morasses-rather more than 150. The Austrians lost 5,922 in killed, wounded, and prisoners.—The battle, it is thought, might have been won by the Russians, if they had fought either before the 1st of December, and consequently before Bernadotte and Davoust had reinforced the French army, or after the 15th; for an army of 80,000 men was approaching, from the Hungarian frontier, towards Vienna and the Danube, led by the Archdukes Charles and John, who had joined their forces near Windisch-Feistritz, in Styria, while Massena re mained at his post on the Isonzo. Troops moreover had been levied in Hungary; a body of Russians, 1,200 strong, under the command of Bennigsen, had invaded Upper Silesia, and prepared the people of Bohemia to rise in a body; and, in consequence of the treaty of Potsdam, Nov. 3, by which the king of Prussia joined the Russian alliance, an army of 180,000 men-Prussians, Saxons, and Hessians-stood ready, in case Napoleon should refuse the mediation of Prussia, according to the treaty of Luneville, to invade France, and to break through Napoleon's line upon the Danube; while an army of 80,000 men-Prussians, Russians, Swedes, Hanoverians, and English, in Upper Germany, threatened the frontiers of the Netherlands. In Italy, too, the landing of the English and Russians might have effected an important diversion. But in spite of all these resources Austria asked for peace. On the 4th Dec., the Emperor Francis himself had a two hours' interview with Napoleon at the French outposts, not far from the village of Nasedlowicz. Napoleon's adjutant, General Savary, accompanied the German emperor back to headquarters, to learn whether Alexander would accede to the treaty. The Russian accounts say that the emperor would not allow him to come into his presence; but the French bulletins give a circumstantial account of his audience, which is also mentioned by the Austrian general Von Stutterheim, the author of 'Materiaux pour servis à l'Hist, de la Bataille d'Austerlitz.' Berthier and Prince Von Liechtenstien concluded, on the 6th, a truce, according to which the French army was to hold the Austrian circle, Venice, a part of Bohemia and Moravia, and Presburg; the Russian army was to evacuate the territories of the emperor of Austria; no levy was to be made in Bohemia or Hungary, and no foreign army was to enter the states of the house of Austria. On the 7th, Napoleon imposed upon the countries held by his troops a tax of 100,000,000 of francs. Alexander, according to the wishes of the emperor of Austria, drew off his army, though he would not accede to the treaty, but placed his troops in Silesia and Lower Saxony, at the disposal of the king of Prussia. The truce of A. paralyzed the strength of the Austrian monarchy, and broke its former alliances, so that the Prussian minister, Haugwitz,who had come to Vienna in November, that he might act as mediator, but had been anticipated by Napoleon,-finding, in the altered state of affairs that he must either declare open war against the French emperor, or make an alliance with him, concluded, in opposition to his instructions, the treaty of Dec. 15th, by which Prussia exchanged the alliance of Russia for that of France. Austria afterwards subscribed the peace of Presburg, by which she not only gave up a territory of 24,200 sq. m., with 2,785,000 inhabitants, and a revenue of 13,610,000 florins, but lost her alliance with Switzerland and Italy, and her influence in the German empire. Thus Napoleon's superiority was established in Italy, the dependence of the princes of Lower Germany upon France confirmed, and Prussia drawn from its system of neutrality.

AUSTERLITZ, a town of Holland, in the prov. and 7 m. E of Utrecht, and 6 m. SSW of Amersfort. -Also a township in the state of New York, U. S., in Columbia co., 31 m. SSW of Albany. It presents a generally hilly surface, and is watered by the Green river. The soil consists of gravel and clay loam. Pop. in 1840, 2,091.
AUSTHORPE, a township in the parishes of Whitkirk and Garforth, W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. E by N of Leeds. Area 660 acres. Pop. 173.
